nodes.title: Note: Color Correction
widgets_values: LoRA's and other settings can cause desaturation, this "Color Correct" node provides a bit of extra 'pop'. Bypass this if things look accurate.

nodes.title: Note: Image Sharpen
widgets_values: I find a little sharpening is nice, but i2v can end up over-sharp if the input image was very sharp to begin with. Consider bypassing.

nodes.title: Note: fp8 versus fp16
widgets_values: Some T2V LoRA's only work with the fp8 scaled model, and Kajai's seem to be better. See the note on model precision for more technical info.

nodes.title: Green Nodes
widgets_values: Stuff you want to change often. The "main controls", if you will. (Also notes about changes.)

nodes.title: Red Nodes
widgets_values:
Important, and not generally changed: 
CLIP / VAE / Negative Prompt / FPS Switching / TripleK Strat

nodes.title: Note: Film Grain
widgets_values: A little film grain improves the general impression of video by adding subtle motion.

nodes.title: Purple Nodes
widgets_values: Conditional nodes that you may want to disable

nodes.title: Note: Variability
widgets_values:
The kSampler node, the resolution, the LoRA's, the diffusion model, the clip model, the prompt, the CFG.

The seed too, obviously, but it's one of the factors you have more control over when attempting to make changes.

The prompt is often quite sensitive to change, small changes can be made, but often even a word or two can make a big change in the results.

I want to call out the sampler node specifically, because it's not something you may have considered, but each sampler is implemented differently and introduces changes. For example, using the ClownsharKsampler will give completely different results than the Ksampler node, even with the same settings.

nodes.title: Note: "Quality"
widgets_values:
Quality comes from steps / sampler for t2v. For i2v, you get to cheat a bit because a very high quality input image for a start frame will help improve the overall quality of the results with less steps.

Poor LoRA quality and LoRA conflicts can lower quality.

Quality can also be improved by choosing a more common aspect ratio. Wider is often sharper and more "cinematic".

nodes.title: Note: Image Size
widgets_values:
Choose from a menu of sizes at an aspect ratio.
If an image is enabled, it will automatically uses the image's aspect ratio (or the closest it can. You can still override! "official_only" should be used when you do T2V for the best results.

nodes.title: Note: Steps
widgets_values:
So normally, people do 4 steps with lightx2\ning. This will work, BUT I have found that 10 steps gives really good results. 8 works too. I2V can go lower, if you want.

You should experiment, but I've left it at the higher value, because the results are reliably good!

nodes.title: Note: What? Is? This?
widgets_values: lightx2/ning are a way to enable faster 4-step generations. These come at the cost of prompt adherance and expressive capability: using these will reduce what Wan 2.2 is capable of, but in exchange you'll still see very good results (and more of them)! I recommend keeping these active, but you should also try setting the acceleration to 'none' to see some of the infinite well of differences.

nodes.title: Reminder:
widgets_values: Adjust lightx2\ning LoRA if you want to. I find this doesn't have much impact on quality.

nodes.title: Note: Model Precision
widgets_values:
| Format / Mode | Bit Width / Structure | Rank (Higher → Lower Precision) | Advantages / When Good | Disadvantages / Trade-offs |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Base Precision Formats** | | | | |
| FP32 | 32-bit float | **1 (highest)** | Very high accuracy; large dynamic range; minimal numerical issues. | High VRAM, slower compute. |
| BF16 | 16-bit float (same exponent bits as FP32, fewer mantissa) | **2** | Close dynamic range to FP32; good trade-off of precision vs memory. | Less fraction precision; possible rounding errors. |
| FP16 | 16-bit float (IEEE half precision) | **3** | Much lower memory; good speed; widely supported. | Less dynamic range; more risk of underflow/overflow; less precision. |
| **Quantization / FP8 / Lower Precision Variants** | | | | |
| FP8 E4M3 (scaled, non-fast / "fp8_e4m3fn_scaled") | 8-bit float: 1 sign, 4 exponent bits, 3 mantissa | **4** | Better precision for small values; useful detail close to zero; decent dynamic range. | Lower dynamic range; need calibration / scaling; possibly slower if fallback. |
| FP8 E5M2 (scaled) | 8-bit float: 1 sign, 5 exponent bits, 2 mantissa | **5** | More dynamic range (can handle bigger/smaller extremes) with some calibration; less error in wide distribution. | Less precision in mantissa; subtle detail around small values may be lost. |
| FP8 E4M3 / E5M2 (fast / non-scaled) | Same structures but with fewer calibrations / optimizations | **6** | Faster; less memory / compute overhead; may still be usable. | More quantization error; possible artifacts; more numerical instability. |
| Q8 / Int8 (integer quantization) | 8-bit integer (no floating exponent) | **7 (lowest)** | Very compact; often fastest; works well if perfect fidelity not required. | Significant loss of precision; limited dynamic range; visible errors in many cases. |

nodes.title: Note: CLIP Models
widgets_values:
| Rank | Text Encoder | Strengths | When to Use / Trade-offs |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | `umt5_xxl_fp16` | Highest fidelity among the three; better detail, more stable text encoding; fewer quantization artifacts. | Use when you have enough VRAM and want the best quality. Ideal for intricate prompts, small text, or detailed scenes. |
| **2** | `umt5_xxl_enc_bf16` | Very close to FP16 in many cases; good dynamic range; often a bit lighter on memory/compute compared to FP16. | Good compromise when FP16 is pushing limits of memory or speed but you still want strong quality. |
| **3** | `umt5_xxl_fp8_e4m3fn_scaled` | Smallest memory footprint; “scaled” helps reduce some FP8 loss; still decent quality if you can accept slight degradation. | Use when memory or speed constraints make FP16/BF16 difficult; acceptable for many tasks where subtle precision is less critical. |

nodes.title: Wan 2.2 Prompt Keywords and Phrases
widgets_values:
[https://wan-22.toolbomber.com/](https://wan-22.toolbomber.com/)

Camera descriptions work best in landscape mode. The effects are less strong in vertical frames.

### Camera distance
* extreme close-up shot - eyes only
* close-up shot - head only
* medium shot - head and upper body only
* medium wide shot - medium shot of subject in wide angle frame
* wide shot - shot of subject from a distance
* wide-angle lens - shows more of the scene in a wide, distant shot
* wide lens - similar to wide-angle with slightly distorted wide view

### Camera height / viewpoint
* over-the-shoulder shot - camera is behind one of the people in frame, at shoulder level
* high angle shot - camera is above the subject, looking down at subject
* low angle shot - camera is below eye level of subject, usually about waist height
* extreme low angle shot - camera is below waist level of subject
* dutch angle shot - camera frame is tilted at an angle so left or right side is higher than the other
* aerial shot - camera is high above the subject, at a distance
* eye-level / waist-level / etc. - places camera height relative to subject

### Camera movement
* camera pushes in for a close-up - camera view gets closer to subject
* camera pulls back- camera view pulls away from subject
* camera pans to the right/left - camera strafes right or left while pointing in same direction
* camera tilts up/down - camera stays in place but tilts its view up or down
* tracking shot - camera moves with the subject to preserve framing
* arc shot - camera rotates around the subject, keeping subject in frame
* handheld camera - camera is shaky and unsteady, moving wildly during scene

### Lighting
* edge lighting - edges of objects are highlighted
* top lighting / side lighting / underlighting - where is the light source relative to subject
* silhouette lighting - light is behind subject, subject is a dark sillhouette
* low contrast lighting - lighting is balanced across the whole frame
* high contrast lighting - some parts of frame are much lighter or darker than others
* dawn/sunrise/afternoon/night time - dictates color and warmth of light
* hard lighting - details are in sharp relief because of lighting
* soft lighting - details are smoothed with softer light effect
* overcast lighting - low contrast, less color saturation
* moonlight - dim, cool color lighting
* daylight - bright sunshing lighting, also use "sunny lighting"

### Colors
* warm colors - trending towards reds, oranges, yellows, more intense colors
* cool colors - trending towards blues, greys, muted colors
* saturated colors - more intense, vibrant colors
* desaturated colors - less intense colors with a more even tone

nodes.title: Note: Length
widgets_values:
The further past 5 seconds you go, the weirder your video might get, and the less dynamic it will seem. Wan is generally trained on 5 second clips, so the best looking clips will be 5 seconds, generally. (Not always true, of course.) Issues with color, quality, and prompt adherance are likely to emerge the further above 5 you go. 

The slider in this workflow stops at 10 seconds for that reason; not only does do longer videos take more proprotional time, but they get worse.

# The Codex
## A Library of notes collected from other workflows.

nodes.title: Note: Clean VRAM / Cache
widgets_values: If you start hitting VRAM errors during long sessions, consider un-bypassing these.

nodes.title: Note: Update LoRA's
widgets_values:
press r: 
You can update the the LoRA's and models available without browser refreshing ComfyUI.

nodes.title: Note: FP16 Accumulation?
widgets_values: This workflow doesn't use FP16 accumulation. With the prevalence of self-forcing, further "compression" to the calculation space is not something I am crazy about. I need to test it more, but I haven't loved the issues I've seen in what I've done. It would be easy to add here if you want maximum speed tho.

nodes.title: Note: NAG?
widgets_values: When CFG is 1, negative prompting is essentially ignored. NAG is a technique enable negative prompting with acceleration LoRA's.

nodes.title: Note: Pinning
widgets_values:
press p: 
Will toggle for selected node. The red pins are used to keep stuff from getting dragged or expanded with the button on the top left.

nodes.title: Note: Copy/Paste Last Frame
widgets_values:
You can actually copy this to be the first frame or the last frame. Want to make a longer video? You can use this trick to do it: 

- Right click the preview, select 'Copy (Clipspace)'. 

- You can then right click on either Start Image Loader or End Image Loader and right click to 'Paste (Clipspace)'. 

Unfortunately, this technique has lots of problems, I talk about on the workflow description page on CivitAI.

nodes.title: Video codec, CRF and features
widgets_values:
### 🎥 H.265 (MP4) CRF Recommendations for WAN 2.2 + RIFE
---

| Purpose | CRF | Notes |
|----------|-----|-------|
| Master / Archival | 19 | Nearly lossless; best for grading or editing |
| Final Render | 20–22 | Ideal quality-to-size balance |
| Web / Social | 23–25 | Lighter files, still decent |
| Previews / Tests | 25–27 | Quick generation, visible compression |

---


💡 *Tip:* For AI-generated videos, CRF 19-21 gives extreme good results with minimal size. H265 is in most every way superior to H264 - better compression, better motion estimation, better colors.

---

nodes.title: Note: Mystical Sources
widgets_values:
I don't know where all of these came from, but will credit whom I can:

Darksidewalker

nodes.title: Note: Last Frame
widgets_values: As a quirk of the Fast Muter that toggles I2V and T2V, it unbypasses this node if it is included in the I2V group. I find this annoying, so I've decided to move this outside of the group. Be sure to toggle this off during T2V if it's active.

nodes.title: Positive Prompt
widgets_values: The women in black sparkling evening gowns do a choreographed dance on a musical film, film studio sound stage set, large art deco staircase prop, a 1920's Golden Age Hollywood film style, cinematic, Busby Berkely style musical. 30 synchronized dancers in glittering gowns dance down the symetrical curved stair cases under art deco chandeliers. The camera pushes in for an extreme close-up of the lead dancer in front who smiles seductively as she dances and sings. The cutest beautiful blonde lead dancer with styled voluminous hair is wearing a sparking gold gown with a mink shawl. The background is a high concept constructed stage set with strong geometric patterns.

nodes.title: Negative Prompt (ignored with CFG 1)
widgets_values: 色调艳丽，过曝，静态，细节模糊不清，字幕，风格，作品，画作，画面，静止，整体发灰，最差质量，低质量，JPEG压缩残留，丑陋的，残缺的，多余的手指，画得不好的手部，画得不好的脸部，畸形的，毁容的，形态畸形的肢体，手指融合，静止不动的画面，杂乱的背景，三条腿，背景人很多，倒着走

nodes.title: Note: Shift
widgets_values:
5.0 is also a popular value. 

Can think of this as: "How much is the scene allowed to evolve?"

nodes.title: Note: Seed Numbers
widgets_values:
There is a common misconception amongst new users: "What's a good seed number?" 

Seed numbers generally do not give you much control. 

There can be useful seeds for some circumstances: for example, two images with the very similar composition will give you similar results for the same prompt with the same seed. 

Additionally, seeds that are close numerically tend to give more similar results. (So if some tiny thing is wrong, it can be fruitful to try going up or down a tic.)

But in most scenarios, it's just randomness. So don't spend too much time testing seeds.

nodes.title: Width and height from aspect ratio 🪴
Note: Low only
widgets_values: You can also try cutting the total high noise steps when running the "Low only" acceleration profile. Unbypass the node to give this as shot. It works well!

nodes.title: Note: Trim Frames
widgets_values: Image-to-video processes often have an "adjustment" period where the model is determining what to do with the images. It can improve the look to remove them under some circumstances.

nodes.title: Note: 🎞️ Interpolators
widgets_values:
Interpolation adds frames to make things look smooth.

GIMM-VFI seems to be the fastest+best quality interpolater. It provides identical results to FILM-VFI in significantly less time. Unlike RIFE, does not produce any notable visual artifacts.

nodes.title: Note: T2V size
widgets_values: It's a good idea to enable "official_only" on the size node for best results, but I won't make this automatic to give you the control.

nodes.title: Note: Which version?
widgets_values: There are many versions of these accelerators that are worth exploring. Each version gives you a different look and results. You'll find some combinations of models and speed LoRA's work better than others.

nodes.title: Note: Strength
widgets_values: You can adjust the strengths here. 2:1 is a popular high to low ratio. 1:1 is fine, but 2 in high noise makes it converge faster. I've seen 2.2:0.8 as well. Some argue to never go above 1:1. Lower the high noise strength can reduce slow motion.

nodes.title: Version Notes:
widgets_values:
# Yet Another Workflow
### v 0.50 beta (MoE) - [Runpod Setup Guide and Install](https://civitai.com/articles/21844/yet-another-workflow-step-by-step-with-runpod-template-v050)
- MoE edition: WanMoeKSampler replaces the sampler stacks; the Sampling Planner is adapted through the new MoE Sampler Breakout (plan-solved shift + steps, lightx via the Acceleration Selector as in standard)
- Added more pullouts for important controls
- Added new scheduler nodes to optimize generations and routing
- Added frame trim to the post processing

nodes.title: OMG. So many wires!
widgets_values: Yes, but I've take this approach because it means you can follow the logic chains. get/set nodes makes things very clean, but it becomes tricky to find what-connects-to-what. In general, you can pilot the workflow with the links hidden.

nodes.title: Note: What is this version?
widgets_values: The intention of the MoE version is to provide similar results to the standard v0.50 edition but with less nodes: the WanMoeKSampler replaces the three sampler stacks and handles the high/low expert handoff internally at the boundary sigma. I suggest changing to the standard version when you're comfortable with this version for more options.

nodes.title: Note: Notable Callouts for Changes in v0.50
widgets_values:
As the changes necessitate packing things a bit tighter, I'm consolidating change notes here. A number of "major" layout changes have occurred since the last release v0.39, and this is always a minor risk to "muscle memory", so I choose this carefully.

I've decided to place more of the plumbing behind other nodes to save space. However, those node groups are now more clearly labeled and organized should you need to access them.

This corresponds to add my new ComfyUI-SamplingPlanner custom node set which routes the signals and optimizes the sigmas (scheduler values for denoising) and model shift based on T2V and I2V and step counts.

Also since the last release, the ComfyUI-WanResolutions node has been updated with better sizing based on additional testing and validation.
